title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee held its regular June 8, 2026 meeting, conducting a public hearing on a special assessment for driveway approach construction at the Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue roundabout intersection project, and approving multiple resolutions and contracts related to ongoing capital improvement projects. Key actions included approving special assessment authorizing resolutions for both the Bladel Avenue and Alderson/Jelnick projects, awarding topsoil screening and paving contracts, and approving a right-of-way services contract amendment and a change order for the Alderson/Jelnick intersection project. Residents near the Alderson/Jelnick intersection, which closed the morning of the meeting, can expect construction through mid-August 2026.

agenda.item: Call to order, Pledge of Allegiance, roll call
Item 6 - Public hearing: Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project special assessment for driveway approach construction
Item 7 - Approval of 5/11/26 Public Works and Utility Committee minutes
Item 8 - Approval of 5/28/26 Bladel Avenue special assessment hearing minutes
Item 9 - Acknowledge May 2026 water and sewer permits
Item 13 - Resolution 2026-016: Approve 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report (CMAR) for Weston Sewer Utility for submission to Wisconsin DNR
Item 14 - Resolution 2026-017: Authorizing resolution for Bladel Avenue (Pine Street to Alderson Street) reconstruction special assessments
Item 15 - Resolution 2026-018: Authorizing resolution for Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project special assessments
Item 18 - Contract amendment number one with MSA for Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue right-of-way services
Item 19 - Change order number one for Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project
Items 20-21 - Future meeting dates and future agenda topics including water main replacement and village lighting

discussions.item: Public Hearing: Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue special assessment for driveway approach construction
discussions.body: Village Engineer Michael presented the project overview for the planned compact roundabout replacing the four-way stop at Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue, with a total estimated project cost of approximately $1.08 million ($900K construction, $180K engineering). Only two private properties are subject to special assessment for driveway approaches, with an assessment rate of $8.61 per square foot — Legacy LLC at $1,747.83 for 203 sq ft and McDonald at $1,549.80 for 180 sq ft. Committee members engaged in extended discussion about the large cost discrepancy between the $8.61/sq ft rate on this project versus $5.40/sq ft on the Bladel Avenue project, attributed to the smaller number of driveway approaches (3 vs. approximately 40) and different contractors (Hos Construction vs. Melvin). The committee consensus, led by Chair Hushang, was to continue using actual bid prices rather than a standardized rate, as setting a fixed rate could shift costs unfairly to village taxpayers. No property owners attended the public hearing. The chair closed the hearing with no further public comment.

discussions.item: Item 7 - Approval of 5/11/26 Public Works and Utility Committee minutes
discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the May 11, 2026 meeting minutes. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote with no discussion.

discussions.item: Item 8 - Approval of 5/28/26 Bladel Avenue special assessment hearing minutes
discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the May 28, 2026 Bladel Avenue special assessment hearing. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote with no discussion.

discussions.item: Item 9 - Acknowledge May 2026 water and sewer permits
discussions.body: A motion was made by the chair and seconded to acknowledge the May 2026 water and sewer permits. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote with no discussion.

Engineer Michael provided a detailed update. On Weston Avenue, pavement settlement in sanitary sewer trenches from fall construction is being repaired — gravel patches have been placed and concrete curb and gutter repour was expected later in the week, with paving to follow. Committee member Hushang attributed the settlement to poor compaction by the contractor, which Michael partially attributed to the 20-foot depth and blasted rock conditions during mid-October construction. The Alderson/Jelnick intersection closed at 7 a.m. the morning of the meeting, with Hos Construction targeting mid-August completion and an incentive in place for early finish. Detours are in place via Park Ridge Avenue or Alterie/Newport. Bladel Avenue project is underway with tree clearing and temporary water setup. Concord and Bayberry project bids are opening June 17. Well 4 GAC tanks have been pumping since October; Well 3 iron/manganese treatment plant addition slab pour was scheduled for the following day. Bellwood Avenue water main project had a bid opening the following Wednesday. Hinter Springs subdivision had concrete deficiencies identified in a walk-through with Denyion that must be corrected before letter of credit sign-off.

Street operations supervisor reported on spring activities including street sweeping, curbside spring cleanup (requiring five staff across leaf bag and brush pickup operations), in-house crack sealing, fixing broken water stacks in the annexed Rothschild area, top soiling and hydro seeding at Yellow Banks, and milling and repaving around Sandy Lane. Shop repairs covered multiple units including bearings and seals on Unit 404, gearbox and driveshaft on Unit 1913, and carburetor replacement on Unit 22. Michael noted the wide range of funding sources — storm water utility, refuse/recycling fund, general fund, water and sewer funds, and parks — reflected in a single month's street crew work.

Utility Manager Josh reported that Well 2 Carrie Well rehab is nearly complete, awaiting a new pump with zinc sleeves and epoxy coating to improve longevity. Unidirectional water main flushing began in May in the northeast portion of the village. A sewer lateral was bored through by what is believed to be Charter Communications, and discussions are ongoing. Messelix lift station cleaning was completed with crew starting at 4 a.m. on the previous Thursday to reduce flows. An INI (inflow and infiltration) study with InPipe is expected to be scheduled by end of June, with the goal of identifying sources of high wet-weather flows — total wastewater flows dropped from 53.8 million gallons in April to 37.5 million in May. GIS integration with IT Pipes televising data is nearing completion.

discussions.item: Item 13 - Resolution 2026-016: 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report (CMAR) for Weston Sewer Utility
discussions.body: Josh noted the village typically earns top marks on the self-evaluation report due to no overflows or backups. A motion was made and seconded to adopt the resolution. During discussion, a typographical error was identified in the fourth 'whereas' clause — the word 'no' was missing, reading incorrectly as 'there are immediate critical problems.' A committee member moved to amend the resolution to correct the text to 'there are no immediate critical problems.' The amendment was seconded and approved unanimously. The original motion as amended then passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Item 14 - Resolution 2026-017: Bladel Avenue reconstruction special assessment authorizing resolution
discussions.body: Michael confirmed no changes from the May 28 public hearing report. The assessment rate for concrete driveway approaches is $5.40 per square foot. A motion was made and seconded to recommend the village board approve Resolution 2026-017. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Item 15 - Resolution 2026-018: Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project special assessment authorizing resolution
discussions.body: The assessment rate of $8.61 per square foot, as discussed in the earlier public hearing, was affirmed with no recommended changes. Chair Hushang stated 'we should go with what is cost — it is what it is.' A motion was made and seconded to recommend the village board approve Resolution 2026-018. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

Two quotes were received: PGA Inc. at $5.50/cubic yard (total $38,500 for estimated 7,000 cubic yards) and Warden Enterprises at $6.50/cubic yard. Michael noted the village saves approximately $100,000 or more annually by screening and reusing its own topsoil versus purchasing contractor-supplied material at approximately $20/yard. The village has an estimated 25,000 cubic yards of unscreened topsoil stockpiled. A motion was made and seconded to award the project to PGA Inc. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

Two quotes were received for 3-inch asphalt paving: RC Pavers at $48,500 and American Asphalt at $56,465, against a budget estimate of $52,855. The overall 2026 surface maintenance budget remains under budget at $842,654 versus an initial estimate of $863,190, leaving approximately $57,000 contingency. Finance Director Troutman advised pausing additional spending to monitor fuel costs. In response to a question about quality, Michael stated RC Pavers has done good work in the village, citing a Kalen Avenue overlay from about 12 years ago that has far exceeded its expected lifespan. A motion was made and seconded to award the project to RC Pavers at $48,500. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Item 18 - MSA right-of-way services contract amendment number one for Alderson/Jelnick project
discussions.body: The original contract with MSA for right-of-way services was $28,425 based on three appraisals and three property acquisitions. During final design, additional property impacts were identified east of the roundabout, including a condo association with four separate property owners requiring individual title work and mortgage company approvals. The amendment adds $24,425, bringing the total contract to $52,850. Michael noted that completing the acquisition within a four-to-five month window — compared to over a year for the River Trail Estates condo acquisition for the Ryan Street lift station project — was a significant achievement. A motion was made and seconded to recommend village board approval. The motion passed unanimously, with one member noting they would abstain [noted after the vote].

discussions.item: Item 19 - Change order number one for Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project
discussions.body: Michael explained that the bid documents inadvertently omitted a bid line item for 24-inch storm pipe that was shown on the plans, only including 12-inch pipe pricing. The change order adds $17,535 to correct this omission. Michael confirmed that even with this addition, the low bidder (Hos Construction) remains the lowest bidder by approximately $80,000 compared to the second-lowest bidder, and the total remains within the storm water utility budget. Unit pricing of $120–$130 per linear foot for 24-inch concrete pipe was validated against City of Wausau bid tabs. Hushang quipped the committee should tell the contractor 'we don't give him any more change orders.' A motion was made and seconded to recommend the board of trustees approve the change order increasing project cost by $17,535. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Items 20-21 - Future agenda topics: water main replacement and village lighting
discussions.body: Michael noted approximately 10 miles of water main replacement remain, even with ongoing projects, and the village is pursuing CDBG grant funding. On village intersection lighting, the committee discussed whether to proactively identify intersections needing lights. Hushang said he would take nighttime photos of intersections to evaluate. The consensus was that staff should act when complaints are received rather than proactively adding lights, as some residents prefer dark skies. The only recent complaint resulted in a light being added at Weston Avenue and County Road J. Michael will add intersection lighting back to the July agenda for further discussion, and Scoffield Avenue lighting was specifically mentioned as a continuation item.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair called for public comment at the start of the meeting and also checked for anyone attending online; no one responded. No property owners subject to the Alderson/Jelnick special assessment attended the public hearing.
actionItems: Approved: May 11, 2026 Public Works and Utility Committee minutes
Approved: May 28, 2026 Bladel Avenue special assessment hearing minutes
Acknowledged: May 2026 water and sewer permits

Adopted: Resolution 2026-016 approving the 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report for Weston Sewer Utility for submission to Wisconsin DNR, amended to correct typographical error in fourth 'whereas' clause
Recommended to village board: Resolution 2026-017 authorizing special assessments at $5.40/sq ft for Bladel Avenue reconstruction driveway approaches
Recommended to village board: Resolution 2026-018 authorizing special assessments at $8.61/sq ft for Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project driveway approaches
Awarded: 2026 topsoil screening project to PGA Inc. at $5.50/cubic yard, estimated $38,500 total
Awarded: Shirley Avenue paving project to RC Pavers at $48,500
Recommended to village board: MSA right-of-way services contract amendment number one for Alderson/Jelnick project, adding $24,425 for a total contract of $52,850
Recommended to village board: Change order number one for Alderson/Jelnick intersection project increasing cost by $17,535 to address omitted 24-inch storm pipe bid item
Staff directed to coordinate concrete, asphalt, and line painting work on Scoffield Avenue to minimize multiple traffic control setups
Staff directed to follow up with American Asphalt regarding manhole repairs on Weston Avenue between Alderson and Birch Streets
Staff directed to add intersection lighting back to July 2026 agenda, including Scoffield Avenue lighting discussion
Next regular meetings: July 13, 2026 and August 10, 2026 at 4:30 p.m.

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to hold its regular meeting on June 8, 2026, with significant financial matters including the preliminary 2026-27 budget and a debt defeasance resolution for 2022 bonds. The board was also expected to consider multiple policy updates, employee handbook changes, meal price adjustments, and a facilities project at Riverview, along with receiving updates on summer learning programs and changes to the DPI Community Eligibility Provision program.

agenda.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
Resolution Authorizing Transfer of Funds and Defeasance of 2022 Bonds
Request for Closed Session - Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation

discussions.body: Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in approximately 2 minutes. This item was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting and was expected to come before the board for action.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in approximately 2 minutes. The presentation was expected to include a marked-up version showing suggested edits and a clean version with changes accepted for board review and approval.

Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in approximately 2 minutes. The item was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting and included supporting documentation including a PLE Tool analysis.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in approximately 2 minutes. This item was previously reviewed at the May Committee Meeting and was expected to be considered for board action.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in approximately 2 minutes. The policy revision was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 concerning the High School Activities Code in approximately 2 minutes. This item was previously reviewed at the May Committee Meeting.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project for RFP approval in approximately 2 minutes. The project was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting.

discussions.item: Resolution Authorizing Transfer of Funds and Defeasance of 2022 Bonds
discussions.body: Elizabeth Channel and the PMA team were scheduled to present the 2026-27 debt defeasance strategy in approximately 10 minutes. The presentation was expected to seek board adoption of a resolution authorizing the transfer of funds, establishment of an escrow account, and defeasance of certain General Obligation School Building and Facility Improvement Bonds, Series 2022, with official defeasance language provided by Quarles & Brady.

Dr. Rob Phelps and Mrs. Vickie Christianson were scheduled to give a brief 5-minute summary of the Summer Learning Program in the Wausau School District. This was an informational presentation with no action requested.

Tiffany Miskowski was scheduled to share a brief 5-minute update on recent changes to the DPI Community Eligibility Provision (CEP) program. This was an informational item regarding state-level program changes.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Action was requested for Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Action was requested for 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Action was requested for Activities & Athletics Code changes
Action was requested for Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
Action was requested for Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code
Action was requested for Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project
Action was requested for Resolution Authorizing Transfer of Funds and Defeasance of 2022 Bonds
Board was expected to consider entering closed session regarding potential litigation under State Statute 19.85(g)

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the committee chair can be present, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. The meeting included public comment critical of the dog day proposal and questions about Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail discussion

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the previous meeting. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from 2023 to present, including the May 9th ribbon cutting for youth baseball fields. Sean Oster explained that the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for lights on the upper field, with approximately $85,000 at the community foundation and a goal of $117,000. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds, which are currently not earmarked pending fundraising progress. A committee member noted the Friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. Discussion emphasized that master plans are guidance documents subject to change and that the 10-year timeline allows flexibility.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and wood mat installation over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats are now village property with extras available for other projects. Committee members asked clarifying questions about the 8x8 wood mats and installation process.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee decided to defer this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The resident who spoke during public comment against the proposal was informed his objection was noted. No formal motion was required for deferral.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Discussion focused on challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches on the trail. Staff explained that pressure washer access is limited due to bridge weight restrictions and terrain. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and potentially organizing volunteer workdays. Committee members suggested community groups might help with maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan presented version 7 of signage for kayak launches along the Eau Claire River. Signs will be installed at three Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks) plus Schofield locations. The Marathon County Parks Foundation is funding the signs through state grants. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. A motion to approve installation was made and seconded, passing unanimously.

Staff proposed adding food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving a request from a community member. The committee agreed to bring it back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and noted the June 11th open house for the bike and pedestrian plan.

publicComment: Two residents provided public comment. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), appearing online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as preventing public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and demanding to know where grant money and glow event funds are located.
actionItems: Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation at village locations
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal and prevention options for Eau Claire River Trail
Food allergy awareness signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was cancelled, making Taste and Glow the sole balloon event in the area.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously without opposition.
The minutes from April 21st were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported last year's attendance was estimated at 11,000-12,000 people and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was cancelled due to permit issues. A commissioner noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future decisions. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for improved hotel usage data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, where she met the candidates and the winner Anastasia. She noted the candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese and were impressed with the community. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow representatives to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about parking challenges at the relocated farmers market at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - license renewals and vouchers
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb Lobsinger supported the fee increase, stating it's justified as new families require more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was also approved unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained that this ordinance creates a regional inspection agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and other municipalities. Under this system, when one department inspects a food truck and places a sticker on it, other participating communities accept that inspection rather than requiring separate inspections in each municipality. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected officials handbook. Trustee Beck raised concerns that the policy language didn't match what was discussed at the previous meeting. After discussion, the board voted to strike sentences requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications, leaving a broader policy where members notify the administrator and chair 12 hours in advance, must be on camera, and cannot participate in closed session. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermling and Lobsinger, and the amended policy passed with the same two opposing.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion approved unanimously. The board also agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after being displaced from the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location when the property owner decided to develop that land. Trustee Beck expressed safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and that vendors appreciate having their vehicles with them. The board acknowledged that if no suitable location can be found, the market may need to be suspended. Discussion was informational only with no action taken.

Village Engineer Michael Dolan reported that an engineering study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, which would support keeping the limit at 45 rather than the 35 mph the original designer recommended. He noted construction delayed collecting additional data but will bring the matter back to the board, possibly at the joint meeting.

The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar transactions requiring tracking for stolen merchandise. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the $108,000 cost for outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting as a board member before being officially appointed by the board. He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park funding in the board packet and called for greater administrator accountability.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with passage of Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive plan steering committee joint meeting with plan commission scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspections assistance
Weston Avenue speed limit study to return to board, possibly at joint meeting
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove health/safety justification requirements
Electronics drive rescheduled to June 6th (not May 20th)
Schofield Coin and Hobby license approved pending background check and occupancy permit

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged monthly budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam about the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Ernling made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Daniels. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

A committee member asked about the final TIF numbers and bond payoff timeline. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, with the CDA needing to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt. The bonds are due October 1st.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Daniels made a motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report together. Soaper seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an extensive overview of clerk operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee. She highlighted that 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is done at the clerk's office counter. Discussion included pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs, and the newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage. Committee members discussed potentially revisiting electronic-only newsletter distribution but noted previous attempts were met with resident pushback.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with her arriving at 6 AM the last three Saturdays to help vendors. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some produce vendors. A committee member noted vendors were very happy with the setup and location. Discussion included parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying only the west side of Alderson allows parking while Newport has never had parking restrictions.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging members to reach out to citizens who may want to participate.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair asked for public comments from the audience and online, with no one coming forward.
actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated this summer before October 1st deadline
Clerk's office seeking to hire one more part-time employee
Clerk anticipates requesting chief inspector pay increase from $13 to $15/hour at next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure-related items including a state transportation funding resolution, a recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel for $126,900, and construction service proposals for upcoming road projects. The committee also directed staff to develop $100/day fine language for sanitary sewer discharge violations after learning the village treated 15 million gallons of clear water in April due to illegal sump pump connections.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
2025 Weston Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, a project originally planned for 2029 that will now likely move to 2028. The grant covers about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered upcoming repairs on Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating to address road depressions over sewer trenches, and planned repairs on Schofield Avenue by HOS Construction.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for about a week, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Several culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. The committee discussed the ongoing flooding issues near Crane Meadows and the need for a permanent pump solution rather than mobilizing equipment each time.

Josh reported on a service repair at Caribou Coffee, ongoing well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. The Kathleen lift station was identified as having significant issues from sump pump discharges. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by Inpipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million and net operating income of $482,911 for 2025. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately 6,100 customers total. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term sustainable state transportation funding. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and compared Wisconsin's automobile ownership costs favorably to neighboring states. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. He calculated the village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement options for residents illegally discharging sump pumps into the sanitary sewer system. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Trustee Mumper suggested distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. The committee agreed on $100/day as the starting fine amount. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day passed unanimously.

Michael requested approval for a temporary lateral connection for a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue until the street is reconstructed. The committee added language requiring Radkey Avenue reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend village board approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for the Military Road project. Weston's utility costs are estimated at $94,860 ($6,156 for sanitary sewer, $88,704 for water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend village board approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine
Move Birch Street reconstruction project from 2029 to 2028 due to grant funding
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water treatment at next meeting
Add lighting improvements to Schofield Avenue project scope when scheduled (approximately 2030)
Special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Water main flushing to begin next week with notices going out to residents
Modify Radkey Avenue agreement to include 2029 reconstruction deadline
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission taking steps to protect municipal water supplies and advance the police facility project.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: The commission reviewed the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Staff confirmed the project meets all B2 zoning requirements, setbacks, and building aesthetics. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to accommodate a gas line easement, with the parking lot now positioned over the 36-inch deep pipeline (acceptable to ANR gas company). The building has capacity for 50-60 officers for future growth. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement and potential for northern expansion. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff presented a program for twice-annual inspections of salt storage facilities within wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg Road has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed to reach out to bring it into compliance. The motion to approve the program with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email notification and calendar invites.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via Zoom. He asked the commission to use common sense on landscaping requirements for the new police department to avoid excessive taxpayer costs, referencing the Green Heck Turner building's landscaping standards. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and compliance outcomes.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for Board of Trustees approval with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection program results to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg Road regarding wellhead protection conditional use compliance
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be discussed at future meeting in fall

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on except approval of the April 1st meeting minutes, which passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date confirmation and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavlli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, the new homeless system response position funded by the county board, public health vending machines providing free tick kits and harm reduction supplies, and the Nurse Family Partnership program which just graduated its first cohort of two-year-olds. Administrator Leonard noted the department has about 37.6 FTE and roughly half of their $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant funding from grants and public charges for services.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. He described the department's work in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. The department has approximately 140 FTE and a 2026 budget of over $22 million with about $7 million from tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting many clients served had never had a bank account, credit account, or been on a lease before.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his 'small but mighty' team of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from approximately 5,900 in 2024. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo discussed suicide prevention efforts following 30 suicides in Marathon County in 2025 (7 were veterans), new housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and the PACT Act's impact on expanding presumptive conditions for VA benefits. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and described good working relationships with neighboring counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county regional organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He explained the ADRC serves as the one-stop shop for aging and disability resources and entry point for programs like Family Care and IRIS. Ray highlighted significant financial challenges in the nutrition/Meals on Wheels program, noting federal funding has remained flat since 2024 while food costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing reductions in meals served, service days, and staffing. Marathon County's levy contribution is $413,135.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
Mike Ray (ADRC) to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda items for future meetings
Strategic plan work to be brought to future meetings by Chair Boots
Next meeting confirmed for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Committee deliberation and vote on Green Valley rezoning
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the town of Green Valley. The town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - McMillan
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property. Staff explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make these conforming uses. The town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic system fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about budget impacts. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to property owners not in declared disaster areas. After discussion, Supervisor Sefelt moved to forward the resolution to the county board. Supervisor Drebeck offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, excluding all other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended motion to forward to the county board passed but was noted as not unanimous.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented an overview of solid waste operations, explaining the landfill's 45-year history, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, and upcoming capital projects including an $6 million expansion and leachate treatment facility required by 2032 under new state law. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about once weekly.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. He explained the department operates both county and city park systems under contract, manages 30,700 acres of county forest, and noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million in economic impact from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt acknowledged Tom's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees for septic systems on properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Green Valley rezoning petition forwarded to county board for approval
McMillan rezoning petition forwarded to county board for approval
Staff to track costs and requests related to any fee waivers implemented
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator following Tom Lovelin's retirement

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

publicComment: Not indicated on agenda.
actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

agenda.item: Elect Chairperson and Vice Chairperson for the 2026-2028 term
Establish regular meeting date and time for the 2026-2028 term
Consideration of April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee Minutes
Approving Consent to Transfer, Waiver of Right of First Refusal, Termination of Deed Restrictions and Approval of New Deed Restrictions for 725 S. 84th Avenue

The committee considered the minutes from the April 6, 2026 Regular Economic Development Committee meeting. The outcome of any approval vote is not recorded in the official vote records.

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, taking action on several ordinance amendments and receiving department reports. Key decisions included recommending approval of amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, approving an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on e-motos, approving fire code inspections for mobile food vendors, and authorizing a raze order for an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire EMS, Police
E-bike ordinance in Chapter 82 traffic and vehicles
Request for crosswalk on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire code inspection amendment

discussions.body: Code enforcement officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. She noted some enforcement-suspended cases may move back to pending status as construction season begins. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week and others coming in May and January. Two SROs work on zoning violations and pet licensing during summer. Motion by Chwanka, second by Olsen to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

Brent Tolson confirmed the amendments had been covered in past meetings and this was the final copy. The tree limb heights over streets and walkways were switched to 14 and 10 feet as previously discussed, with language added requiring compliance with the jurisdiction having authority. Motion by Olsen, second by Campkey to recommend approval to the board of trustees passed unanimously.

Discussion focused heavily on e-motos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting e-motos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns including people doing wheelies past pedestrians. Committee debated whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers should also be restricted. Olsen noted he preferred passing a starting ordinance rather than over-regulating. Motion by Olsen, second by Chwanka to recommend approval with the addition that e-motos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is far longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 5-hour windows overnight. Chief Hunt expressed opposition to eliminating overnight parking, saying it gives officers time to clear vehicles before plows arrive. Michael (Public Works) noted they rarely plow before 4-5am and suggested signing specific problem areas instead. Committee discussed uniformity with other municipalities and reducing hours. No action taken; Michael was asked to identify specific exception areas to bring back in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest high school parking lot, which church members use for Sunday parking. Michael estimated basic crosswalk cost at $2,000, lighted system at $25-30,000. Committee expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for private entity requests. Motion by Olsen, second by Chwanka to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive Church covering the installation cost passed unanimously.

Fire Chief explained a regional consortium including Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Safer has formed to share food truck fire inspections - one inspection with sticker is valid for all participating municipalities. City of Wausau opted out to do their own. Chief noted one recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, second by Campkey to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors needing correction: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence due to discovered apartment unit, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing even number assignment. Chief Hunt added another issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight units having separate addresses. Motion to recommend approval to board of trustees passed unanimously.

Structure has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and was deemed too deteriorated for fire department training burn. Owners had housed chickens inside during winter. Current owners prefer the village proceed with demolition, with costs added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, second by Campkey to recommend board authorization for raze order passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass destruction from vehicle parking and questioning whether current village code allows parking on grass. He raised ADA concerns about handicap spaces signed on grass rather than asphalt, noting wheelchair operation is difficult on wet, sandy soil. He questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks when no trucks have burned down. He noted the farmers market moving to public property changes food truck permitting dynamics and the ordinance language is unclear about which days restrictions apply. Finally, he criticized accountability on long-standing code violations, stating some cases have been pending for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for board approval
E-bike ordinance recommended for board approval with e-moto prohibition on all public property added
Michael to identify specific areas needing overnight parking exceptions and bring back in July
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for Delicoski, Ross, and Radkey properties recommended for board approval
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue authorized to proceed
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 p.m.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to consider action on a pupil expulsion matter during or following the closed session hearing

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an organizational meeting approving committee appointments, electing Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously passing a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris cleanup following the April severe weather emergency. The board heard emotional testimony from Ringle residents requesting additional fee relief for tornado and flood damage cleanup.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments and chair remarks on leadership
Election of at-large executive committee members
Ballot counting and announcement of executive committee election results
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees for storm debris

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspectives, developing leadership among newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Vice Chair Boots assisted in developing the appointments. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. Passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. Motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. Motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: County Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board following severe weather damage in Ringle and surrounding areas. The resolution reduces tipping fees from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified storm debris by waiving state fees ($13.10), town fees ($2.33), and reducing to the commercial contract rate. Supervisor Rosenberg requested public comment be allowed. Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck spoke, asking the county to fully waive fees given that the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their portions. Leonard noted the county will likely not meet the $670,743 damage threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle will qualify. The board suspended rules to consider the resolution (passed unanimously), then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously. Administrator Leonard indicated discussions would continue with the Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanisms for landowners.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to fully waive fees for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle have waived their portions, and describing how businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup costs. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for underinsured homeowners and noting some insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived at affected properties.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for landowner out-of-pocket costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Staff to track certified storm debris separately for potential state disaster aid documentation

overview: The Board of Public Works approved minutes from the previous meeting but the proposal for Hand Labor Mowing Services for 2026-2030 failed. The adjournment motion also failed on a 2-0 vote moved by Vincent Bonino and seconded by Eric Lindman.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
